options.markscheme: This describes the mechanical disadvantage of a third-class lever. In the second-class lever of a calf raise, the load is closer to the fulcrum than the effort is.

options.content: The system prioritizes range of motion and speed over force production.
options.markscheme: This is a trait of third-class levers, which are common in the limbs. Second-class levers, such as the ankle during plantar flexion, prioritize the ability to lift heavy loads with less effort.

options.content: A mechanical advantage is gained because the effort arm is longer than the load arm.
options.markscheme: A calf raise functions as a second-class lever. In this system, the effort (calf muscles pulling the heel) is further from the fulcrum (balls of the feet) than the load (body weight through the ankle), resulting in a mechanical advantage ($MA > 1$).

options.content: Equilibrium is achieved by placing the fulcrum directly between the effort and the load.
options.markscheme: This describes a first-class lever arrangement. In a second-class lever used during a calf raise, the load is positioned between the fulcrum and the effort.

options.markscheme: Correct: the atlanto-occipital joint acts as the fulcrum, with the load (weight of the head) and effort (neck extensor/flexor muscles) on opposite sides, forming a first-class lever.
Incorrect: in a second-class lever the load lies between the fulcrum and the effort, which does not describe the atlanto-occipital joint during heading.

options.content: The fulcrum is positioned between the effort and the resistance.
options.markscheme: Incorrect. This describes a first-class lever. In the human body, an example of this is the extension of the neck, where the atlanto-occipital joint acts as the fulcrum.

options.content: The resistance is positioned between the fulcrum and the effort.
options.markscheme: Correct. A standing calf raise is a second-class lever. The ball of the foot (metatarsophalangeal joint) acts as the fulcrum, the body weight (resistance) acts through the ankle joint, and the gastrocnemius provides the effort at the heel.

options.content: The effort is positioned between the fulcrum and the resistance.
options.markscheme: Incorrect. This describes a third-class lever, such as a biceps curl or knee extension, which is the most common lever type in the human body.

options.content: The effort and the resistance are applied at the exact same point on the bone.
options.markscheme: Incorrect. For a lever to function in biomechanics, the effort (muscle insertion) and resistance (load) must be applied at different points relative to the fulcrum (joint).

options.content: They increase joint stability by keeping the load close to the fulcrum throughout movement.
options.markscheme: Incorrect. Third-class levers are not primarily used because they keep the load close to the fulcrum; their key benefit is increased speed and range of movement at the distal end.

parts.content: Explain how joint movement in multiple planes contributes to complex sports actions such as a tennis serve.

questions.specification: In rowing, treating the oarlock (rowlock) as the pivot/fulcrum, the oar acts as which type of lever outside the body?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: B.1 Generating movement in the body > B.1.4 Levers in movement and sport > B.1.4.1 Classes of levers and their applications in human movement and sport
labeledSubSubTopics.title: B.1.4.1 Classes of levers and their applications in human movement and sport
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question asks which class of lever an oar acts as in rowing (a sport application of lever classes), directly matching the topic on classes of levers and their applications in human movement and sport.

options.markscheme: With the oarlock (rowlock) as the fulcrum, the effort is applied by the rower at the handle and the load/resistance is at the blade in the water. The order along the lever is handle (effort) — oarlock (fulcrum) — blade (load), so the fulcrum is between effort and load, which defines a first-class lever.

A second-class lever has the load between the fulcrum and the effort. With the oarlock (rowlock) as the fulcrum, the load/resistance is at the blade in the water (not between the fulcrum and the effort), so this classification is not correct here.

A third-class lever has the effort between the fulcrum and the load. With the oarlock (rowlock) as the fulcrum and the effort applied at the handle, the effort is not between fulcrum and load, so this is not correct.
An oar is a lever and, when treating the oarlock (rowlock) as the fulcrum, it fits the definition of a first-class lever, so this option is not correct.

- The frontal plane contributes during the stabilization phase, particularly in shoulder abduction and maintaining core balance during the overhead jerk <marks>A1</marks>
- The transverse plane is engaged through rotational stability and torque required during the lift and transition phases <marks>A1</marks>
- First-class levers are utilized during head and neck stabilization where neck extensors counterbalance head tilt <marks>A1</marks>
- Second-class levers are involved at the ankle joint during plantarflexion (e.g., raising the heel to generate power during the jerk) <marks>A1</marks>
- Third-class levers dominate in elbow flexion (biceps acting between the load and fulcrum) and hip extension (gluteus maximus) to allow for speed of movement <marks>A1</marks>

parts.content: State one type of muscle tissue found in the human body.
parts.markscheme:

<note>Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for any one correct type.</note>

parts.content: With reference to Figure 1, state two roles of fascia in muscular function during the kick.
parts.markscheme:
- Surrounds/separates muscles to allow independent movement (reduces interference between adjacent muscles) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Transmits force generated by muscle contraction to tendons/bone (force transmission through connective tissue) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Provides structural support/maintains alignment of muscle compartments <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Reduces friction between muscles/acts as a gliding surface between tissues <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Award <marks>2 marks</marks> for any two distinct correct roles.</note>

parts.content: Outline three actions of skeletal muscle relevant to producing the instep kick.
parts.markscheme:
- Produces voluntary movement by contracting and pulling on bones (e.g., knee extension via quadriceps) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Maintains posture/body position against gravity (e.g., trunk/hip stabilization during the kick) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Stabilizes joints/supports the skeletal structure (e.g., dynamic stabilization of the knee) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Generates heat during activity (thermogenesis during muscular work) <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Award <marks>3 marks</marks> for any three distinct correct actions.</note>

parts.content: With reference to the knee in Figure 1, describe two properties of synovial joints that allow movement.
parts.markscheme:
- Synovial fluid reduces friction between the articular surfaces <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Articular cartilage provides a smooth surface and absorbs shock <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Joint capsule/ligaments maintain joint integrity while allowing movement and contain synovial fluid <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Award <marks>2 marks</marks> for any two distinct correct properties.</note>

parts.content: Explain how the quadriceps attachment points shown in Figure 1 influence movement mechanics at the knee joint.
parts.markscheme:
- Origin is typically the more fixed/proximal attachment and insertion is the more movable/distal attachment (during knee extension the tibia moves relative to the femur) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Relative positions of the attachment points determine the line of pull and therefore the direction of movement/torque produced <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Distance of the tendon from the joint axis (moment arm) affects torque and mechanical advantage for a given muscle force <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Greater moment arm (insertion further from the joint axis) increases torque/strength but reduces angular velocity/range for a given shortening <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Smaller moment arm (insertion closer to the joint axis) reduces torque but favours speed and range of motion <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Award <marks>3 marks</marks> for any three linked correct points. Accept other correct biomechanical explanations consistent with Figure 1.</note>

parts.content: Explain how the neuromuscular system coordinates voluntary knee extension during the kick (motor cortex → motor neuron → neuromuscular junction → contraction).
parts.markscheme:
- Motor cortex initiates the signal for voluntary movement <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Action potentials travel along motor neurons to the relevant motor units in the quadriceps <marks>1 mark</marks>
- At the neuromuscular junction, the nerve impulse leads to release of acetylcholine, generating an action potential in the muscle fibre <marks>1 mark</marks>
- The action potential causes calcium release in the muscle fibre, enabling actin–myosin cross-bridge cycling to produce force <marks>1 mark</marks>
- CNS integrates sensory feedback/proprioception to coordinate timing and force for smooth/precise movement <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Award <marks>4 marks</marks> for any four correct, sequentially linked points showing coordination from the CNS to contraction.</note>

questions.specification: In which situation does a first-class lever act at a mechanical disadvantage?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: B.1 Generating movement in the body > B.1.4 Levers in movement and sport > B.1.4.1 Classes of levers and their applications in human movement and sport
labeledSubSubTopics.title: B.1.4.1 Classes of levers and their applications in human movement and sport
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question asks about when a first-class lever operates at a mechanical disadvantage, which depends on the relative positions of fulcrum, effort, and load—core content in classes of levers and their applications in movement/sport.

options.content: When the load is closer to the fulcrum than the effort
options.markscheme: If the load is closer to the fulcrum than the effort, the load arm is shorter than the effort arm, so $MA=\frac{\text{effort arm}}{\text{load arm}}>1$ (mechanical advantage), not disadvantage.

options.content: When the effort is closer to the fulcrum than the load
options.markscheme: Mechanical disadvantage occurs when $MA<1$. For a first-class lever, $MA=\frac{\text{effort arm}}{\text{load arm}}$, so if the effort is closer to the fulcrum than the load, effort arm < load arm and $MA<1$.

options.content: When the fulcrum is equidistant from effort and load
options.markscheme: If the fulcrum is equidistant, effort arm = load arm so $MA=1$ (no mechanical advantage or disadvantage).

options.content: When the load is farther from the fulcrum than the effort
options.markscheme: This describes the same condition as option B (effort closer to fulcrum than load), which would make it correct; therefore it is not an appropriate distractor for a single-correct MCQ.

questions.specification: Which of the following is an example of a first-class lever in sports?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: B.1 Generating movement in the body > B.1.4 Levers in movement and sport > B.1.4.1 Classes of levers and their applications in human movement and sport
labeledSubSubTopics.title: B.1.4.1 Classes of levers and their applications in human movement and sport
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question asks to identify an example of a first-class lever in sports, which directly tests knowledge of lever classes and their application to human movement.

options.markscheme: Biceps curl is a third-class lever (effort applied between the elbow fulcrum and the load in the hand).
Plantarflexion when rising onto the toes is typically a second-class lever (load between the fulcrum at the metatarsophalangeal joints and the effort via the Achilles tendon).
Elbow extension by the triceps is a first-class lever: the elbow joint is the fulcrum between the effort (triceps force) and the resistance/load (forearm/hand or external load).
Knee flexion by the hamstrings is generally a third-class lever (effort applied between the knee fulcrum and the load at the distal leg/foot).

labeledSubSubTopics.title: B.1.4.1 Classes of levers and their applications in human movement and sport

options.content: The muscle must produce more force than the weight of the resistance, but the resistance moves at a higher velocity.
options.markscheme: Correct: third-class levers have mechanical disadvantage (effort arm shorter than resistance arm), so greater muscle force is needed, but they provide increased speed/ROM at the distal segment.

options.content: The muscle produces less force than the weight of the resistance, but the resistance moves a shorter distance.
options.markscheme: Incorrect: this describes a mechanical advantage (typical of second-class levers), not a third-class lever.

options.content: The fulcrum is located between the effort and the resistance to provide maximum stability for the joint.
options.markscheme: Incorrect: fulcrum between effort and resistance describes a first-class lever, not the main trade-off of a third-class lever.

options.content: The effort arm is significantly longer than the resistance arm to provide a mechanical advantage.
options.markscheme: Incorrect: third-class levers have the effort applied between fulcrum and resistance and typically have an effort arm shorter than the resistance arm (mechanical disadvantage).

options.markscheme: Incorrect. In a first-class lever, the fulcrum is positioned between the effort and the resistance (e.g., the neck during head extension).
Correct. A second-class lever is defined by the resistance (load) being located between the fulcrum and the effort, which occurs during plantar flexion (calf raises).

Incorrect. In a third-class lever, the effort is applied between the fulcrum and the resistance (e.g., a bicep curl). While most levers in the body are third-class, the calf raise is a notable exception.
Incorrect. Human anatomical lever systems are categorized into only three classes; there is no fourth-class lever.
